core_meaning: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> nominalizes verbs and adjectives, turning them into abstract 'things' or 'matters' — it refers to facts, situations, and abstract concepts rather than physical objects. Unlike <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>, which refers to tangible things you can touch, <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> captures intangible ideas, actions, and experiences.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>のこと、まだ<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きなんだよね。
sentences.translation: You still have feelings for her, don't you.
sentences.notes: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> here means 'the matter of / everything about' someone — a very natural way to talk about caring for or thinking about a person as a whole.

sentences.jp: <ruby>健康<rt>けんこう</rt></ruby>のために<ruby>運動<rt>うんどう</rt></ruby>することが<ruby>大切<rt>たいせつ</rt></ruby>です。
sentences.translation: Exercising for your health is important.
sentences.notes: This shows the classic grammatical use: verb plain form + <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> nominalizes the action so it can be the subject of the sentence.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>嘘<rt>うそ</rt></ruby>をついていたことは<ruby>明<rt>あき</rt></ruby>らかだ。
sentences.translation: It is clear that he was lying.
sentences.notes: Plain form + <ruby>こと</rt></ruby> functions as a noun clause ('the fact that'), allowing the whole clause to be the subject — a core grammatical pattern.

sentences.jp: <ruby>日本語<rt>にほんご</rt></ruby>を<ruby>毎日<rt>まいにち</rt></ruby><ruby>勉強<rt>べんきょう</rt></ruby>することにしました。
sentences.translation: I have decided to study Japanese every day.
sentences.notes: <ruby>〜することにする</ruby> is a fixed construction meaning 'to decide to do ~' — <ruby>こと</rt></ruby> is essential glue in this pattern.

sentences.jp: <ruby>緊急<rt>きんきゅう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>場合<rt>ばあい</rt></ruby>は、<ruby>直<rt>ただ</rt></ruby>ちに<ruby>係員<rt>かかりいん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>知<rt>し</rt></ruby>らせること。
sentences.translation: In case of emergency, immediately notify a staff member.
sentences.notes: Plain form + <ruby>こと</rt></ruby> at the end of a sentence functions as a written instruction or rule — common on signs, manuals, and official notices.

dont_use: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> should not be used for physical, tangible objects — use <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby> instead (e.g. 'something I bought' is <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>ったもの, not <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>ったこと). Don't confuse it with <ruby>の<rt>の</rt></ruby> nominalization: while both can nominalize verbs, <ruby>の<rt>の</rt></ruby> is more perceptual/direct ('I heard him sing' → <ruby>歌<rt>うた</rt></ruby>うのが<ruby>聞<rt>き</rt></ruby>こえた), while <ruby>こと</rt></ruby> is more abstract. Avoid writing <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> in kanji in grammatical nominalizer roles — in those cases, the hiragana こと is standard and writing the kanji can look unnatural.

confused_with.contrast: A common mistake is saying <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>ったことがたくさんある when you mean 'there are many things I bought' — here you need <ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>ったものがたくさんある because you're referring to actual physical items. <ruby>こと</rt></ruby> is for abstract matters and experiences (e.g. <ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>ったことがある — 'I've had the experience of going'), while <ruby>もの</rt></ruby> is for tangible, concrete things you can point to.

confusion_set.use_when: The 'thing' is abstract — an event, fact, action, or experience you can't hold.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>会<rt>あ</rt></ruby>ったことがある。(I've had the experience of meeting him.)

confusion_set.use_when: The 'thing' is a tangible, physical object you can see or touch.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>冷蔵庫<rt>れいぞうこ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるものがない。(There's nothing to eat in the fridge.)

confusion_set.use_when: Nominalizing a verb when the situation is directly observable or sensory, or in casual speech.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>泣<rt>な</rt></ruby>いているのが<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>えた。(I could see that she was crying.)

frequency: <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> is extremely common across all registers — it appears constantly in daily speech, writing, and grammar patterns. As a grammatical nominalizer it is one of the most essential items at the N4–N3 level. The kanji form <ruby>事<rt>こと</rt></ruby> appears more in formal writing; in grammatical uses (ことがある、ことにする、ことができる), hiragana こと is the standard written form.
anki_hint: Think of こと as an 'abstract bubble' that wraps a verb or idea and turns it into a concept you can talk about — if you can't touch it, reach for こと over <ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>.
